sources.sourceName: OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (answer key for odd-numbered review questions)

meta.notes: Psychology (PSBB) items scraped verbatim from OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch. 9 and Ch. 10 review-questions pages; correct answers from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), odd-numbered review questions only. Ch. 10 Q7 distractor option B is printed as 'msevere obesity' on the source page (apparent typo for 'severe obesity'); reproduced verbatim. Live OpenStax pages show CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 plus a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing/non-AI per PRD.

questions.stem: The view that development is a cumulative process, gradually adding to the same type of skills is known as ________.
questions.explanation: Continuous development views growth as a gradual, cumulative process of adding to the same kinds of skills, in contrast to discontinuous (stage-based) development.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 9 Q1 = C.

hints.prompt: This item contrasts two views of how development unfolds. What does the 'continuous' view claim?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Development is gradual and cumulative, adding to the same kinds of skills.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Development happens in distinct, qualitatively different stages.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Development is driven only by genetics.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Development is driven only by the environment.

hints.rationale: Continuous development is gradual, cumulative growth in the same kinds of skills.

hints.prompt: How does continuous development differ from discontinuous development?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Continuous is smooth and gradual; discontinuous proceeds in distinct stages.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Continuous happens in stages; discontinuous is gradual.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They mean exactly the same thing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Continuous refers to genes; discontinuous to environment.

hints.rationale: Gradual accumulation versus distinct stages is the key contrast.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed choice is the DIRECT opposite of the described view (stage-based development), a tempting distractor?
hints.rationale: Discontinuous (stage-based) development is the opposite of the gradual view, making it the trap.

choices.label: A
choices.text: The study of how we grow and change from conception to death.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The study of how we grow and change in infancy and childhood.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The study of physical, cognitive, and psychosocial growth in children.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The study of emotions, personality, and social relationships.

questions.explanation: Lifespan development is the scientific study of how humans grow, change, and remain stable across the entire life, from conception to death.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 9 Q3 = A.

hints.prompt: This item tests the scope of lifespan development. Which span does it cover?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The entire life, from conception to death.

hints.rationale: Lifespan development spans conception through death.

hints.prompt: How does lifespan development differ from, say, child development?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It studies change across the whole life, not just one period like childhood.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It studies only physical growth in children.

hints.rationale: Its defining breadth is coverage of the entire life.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which answer choice narrows the field to only emotional and social topics, ignoring the full lifespan?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The study of how we grow and change from conception to death.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The study of how we grow and change in infancy and childhood.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The study of physical, cognitive, and psychosocial growth in children.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The study of emotions, personality, and social relationships.

hints.rationale: Choice D lists only some topics and omits the whole-life scope, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: Which theorist proposed that moral thinking proceeds through a series of stages?
questions.explanation: Lawrence Kohlberg proposed a stage theory of moral development (preconventional, conventional, and postconventional levels).
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 9 Q5 = D.

hints.prompt: This item asks who proposed a STAGE theory of MORAL development. What does such a theory describe?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Moral reasoning advancing through an ordered series of stages.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Moral judgment being fixed at birth and never changing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Morality being purely a matter of genetics.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Morality being identical in everyone regardless of age.

hints.rationale: A stage theory of moral development describes reasoning progressing through stages.

hints.prompt: The theory in question describes moral reasoning moving through which progression?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Preconventional, then conventional, then postconventional levels.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A single fixed level for everyone.

hints.rationale: The levels run preconventional to conventional to postconventional.

hints.prompt: Strategy: which detail in the question tells you the answer must be the theorist of MORAL stages specifically?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The phrase 'moral thinking proceeds through a series of stages.'

choices.label: B
choices.text: That the answer is a person's name.

choices.label: D
choices.text: That it is a psychology question.

hints.rationale: The moral-stages phrase is the clue that pins down the right theorist.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the correct order of prenatal development?
questions.explanation: Prenatal development proceeds in the order zygote (germinal), then embryo (embryonic stage), then fetus (fetal stage).
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax (option B printed as 'fetus, embryo zygote'). correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 9 Q7 = D.

hints.prompt: This item tests the sequence of prenatal stages. Which three stages are involved?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The zygote, the fetus, and the embryo.

hints.rationale: Prenatal development involves the zygote, embryo, and fetus stages.

hints.prompt: Which prenatal stage comes FIRST, right after conception?
hints.rationale: The zygote forms first, at conception.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed ordering is clearly wrong because it is fully reversed (fetus first, zygote last)?
hints.rationale: Choice B reverses the true order (fetus first, zygote last), so it is an obvious trap.

questions.stem: What begins as a single-cell structure that is created when a sperm and egg merge at conception?
questions.explanation: The zygote is the single-celled structure formed when a sperm fertilizes an egg at conception.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 9 Q9 = C.

hints.prompt: This item asks for the structure formed at conception. What defines it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A single cell created when sperm and egg merge.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A multi-week-old organism with developing organs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A child that has already been born.

hints.rationale: The structure at conception is a single fertilized cell.

hints.prompt: At the very moment of conception, what has just formed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A single fertilized cell, before any further development.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A fetus in the last months of pregnancy.

hints.rationale: Only one fertilized cell exists at conception.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed choice names the LATEST stage (an unborn baby near birth), not the single cell at conception?
hints.rationale: The fetus is a late prenatal stage, not the single conception cell, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: The child uses the parent as a base from which to explore her world in which attachment style?
questions.explanation: In secure attachment, the child uses the caregiver as a secure base for exploration and is comforted by the caregiver's return.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 9 Q11 = A.

hints.prompt: This item tests attachment styles. The child uses the parent as a base to explore and is comforted on reunion. What does this indicate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A trusting, secure bond with the caregiver.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A bond marked by avoiding the caregiver.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A bond marked by clingy distress and resistance.

hints.rationale: Using the caregiver as a secure base signals a secure bond.

hints.prompt: What most distinguishes the secure style from the insecure styles?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The child uses the caregiver as a safe base and is soothed by their return.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The child ignores the caregiver entirely.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The child cannot be comforted at all.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The child shows no consistent pattern.

hints.rationale: A safe base plus comfort on reunion defines secure attachment.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed style involves AVOIDING or ignoring the caregiver—the opposite of using them as a base?
hints.rationale: Insecure avoidant involves avoiding the caregiver, the opposite of a secure base, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: Who created the very first modern hospice?
questions.explanation: Dame Cicely Saunders founded the first modern hospice (St Christopher's Hospice, London) in 1967, launching the modern hospice movement.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 9 Q13 = B.

hints.prompt: This item concerns the person who launched the modern hospice movement. What was that key achievement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Founding the first modern hospice, starting the hospice movement.

hints.rationale: The relevant achievement is founding the first modern hospice.

hints.prompt: The question is specifically about the FIRST MODERN hospice. Which point matters most for choosing correctly?

choices.label: A
choices.text: You need the founder of the first modern hospice, not a founder of another field.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The exact number of answer choices.

choices.label: C
choices.text: That all the options are names.

choices.label: D
choices.text: That it is a medical topic in general.

hints.rationale: Focusing on 'first modern hospice founder' narrows the choice.

hints.prompt: Strategy: which phrase in the stem most directly points to the correct person?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The phrase 'created the very first modern hospice.'

hints.rationale: The 'first modern hospice' phrase is the discriminating clue.

questions.stem: Need for ________ refers to maintaining positive relationships with others.
questions.explanation: The need for affiliation is the motivation to build and maintain positive relationships and a sense of belonging with others.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 10 Q1 = B.

hints.prompt: This item tests the need for affiliation. What is it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The motivation to build and maintain positive relationships and belonging.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The drive to avoid all social contact.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The drive to win competitions at any cost.

hints.rationale: The need for affiliation is the motive for positive relationships and belonging.

hints.prompt: Someone high in the need for affiliation would most value what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Warm, positive relationships and a sense of belonging.

hints.rationale: Belonging and positive relationships are central to affiliation.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed need is about ACHIEVING and excelling at tasks, not maintaining relationships?
hints.rationale: The need for achievement is about excelling at tasks, not relationships, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: ________ is an individual's belief in her capability to complete some task.
questions.explanation: Self-efficacy is a person's belief in their own capability to perform a specific task or reach a goal.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 10 Q3 = D.

hints.prompt: This item tests self-efficacy. What is it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A person's belief in their capability to perform a specific task.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A general feeling of being liked by everyone.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A guarantee of succeeding at absolutely everything.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A basic bodily need for food and water.

hints.rationale: Self-efficacy is belief in one's capability to complete a task.

hints.prompt: How does self-efficacy differ from general self-esteem?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Self-efficacy is task-specific confidence; self-esteem is overall self-worth.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are exactly the same thing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Self-efficacy is about food and shelter.

hints.rationale: Self-efficacy is specific task confidence, not global self-worth.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed option is about overall self-WORTH rather than task-specific capability?
hints.rationale: Self-esteem is overall self-worth, not task-specific capability, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: According to your reading, nearly ________ of the adult population in the United States can be classified as obese.
questions.explanation: Per the OpenStax text, nearly one third of the U.S. adult population can be classified as obese.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 10 Q5 = B.

hints.prompt: This item asks for a statistic about U.S. adult obesity from the reading. What kind of value is requested?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A fraction of the adult population classified as obese.

hints.rationale: The blank calls for a fraction of adults who are obese.

hints.prompt: Which statement is TRUE about how common adult obesity is, per the reading?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It affects a large share—well under half, but far more than a tiny fraction—of U.S. adults.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It affects only children, not adults.

hints.rationale: The reading describes a substantial but sub-majority share of adults.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed value is clearly TOO HIGH, describing about half of all adults?
hints.rationale: 'One half' overstates the figure, so it is a trap to eliminate.

questions.stem: ________ is characterized by episodes of binge eating followed by attempts to compensate for the excessive amount of food that was consumed.
questions.explanation: Bulimia nervosa is characterized by episodes of binge eating followed by compensatory behaviors (such as purging or excessive exercise).
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax; option B is printed as 'msevere obesity' on the source page (apparent typo for 'severe obesity'). correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 10 Q7 = D.

hints.prompt: This item tests an eating disorder defined by a specific cycle. What is that cycle?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Episodes of binge eating followed by compensatory behavior such as purging.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Never eating any food at all.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Eating only at night with no other pattern.

choices.label: D
choices.text: An intense fear of public speaking.

hints.rationale: Binge eating plus compensation defines this disorder.

hints.prompt: How does this binge-and-compensate disorder differ from severe food restriction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It involves bingeing then compensating, rather than simply restricting intake.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It involves no eating at all.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It involves only weight gain with no behaviors.

hints.rationale: The binge-then-compensate cycle sets it apart from pure restriction.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed option is about severely RESTRICTING food while underweight, not binge-and-compensate?
hints.rationale: Anorexia nervosa centers on food restriction, not the binge-compensate cycle, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: Animal research suggests that in male rats the ________ is critical for the ability to engage in sexual behavior, but not for the motivation to do so.

choices.label: C
choices.text: medial preoptic area of the hypothalamus

questions.explanation: In male rats, the medial preoptic area of the hypothalamus is critical for the ability to perform sexual behavior, though not for the motivation to engage in it.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 10 Q9 = C.

hints.prompt: This item separates the ABILITY to perform sexual behavior from the MOTIVATION for it, in male rats. What is the key idea?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A brain region can control the capacity to perform a behavior separately from the drive to do it.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ability and motivation are always controlled by the same region.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Motivation cannot be studied in animals.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sexual behavior has no brain basis.

hints.rationale: The finding is that performance capacity and motivation can be dissociated.

hints.prompt: According to the finding, the target region is critical for which aspect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The ABILITY to perform the behavior, but not the motivation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The motivation, but not the ability.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both, in a way that cannot be separated.

hints.rationale: The region governs performance ability, not the drive.

hints.prompt: Strategy: which detail in the stem is the key clue for the correct region?

choices.label: A
choices.text: That the region governs the ABILITY to perform, not the motivation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: That four brain regions are listed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: That the item is marked hard.

hints.rationale: The ability-not-motivation detail is the discriminating clue.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings was not a result of the Kinsey study?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sexual desire and sexual ability can be separate functions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Females enjoy sex as much as males.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sexual acts between members of the same gender are fairly common.

questions.explanation: Per the source, the separation of sexual desire from sexual ability was not among the Kinsey study's findings; the other statements reflect findings attributed to Kinsey's work.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 10 Q11 = A.

hints.prompt: This item asks which statement was NOT a finding of the Kinsey study. What must the correct answer be?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A statement that the Kinsey study did NOT actually find.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A statement the study strongly confirmed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A statement true of every study ever done.

hints.rationale: On a 'which was NOT a finding' item, the answer is the non-finding.

hints.prompt: For a 'which was NOT a finding' question, how should you approach the choices?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Identify the three that WERE findings and select the remaining one.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pick the one that sounds most shocking.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Select two answers to be safe.

hints.rationale: Eliminating the genuine findings leaves the exception.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed statement — the one about masturbation — WAS a Kinsey finding, so it can be eliminated as the exception?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sexual desire and sexual ability can be separate functions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Females enjoy sex as much as males.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sexual acts between members of the same gender are fairly common.

hints.rationale: The masturbation statement was a genuine Kinsey finding, so it is not the exception and can be ruled out.

questions.stem: Individuals suffering from posttraumatic stress disorder have been shown to have reduced volumes of the ________.
questions.explanation: Research links PTSD with reduced hippocampal volume, consistent with the hippocampus's role in memory and stress regulation.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 10 Q13 = B.

hints.prompt: This item links PTSD to reduced volume in a brain region tied to memory and stress. Which functions are central to that region?
hints.rationale: The region reduced in PTSD is central to memory and stress regulation.

hints.prompt: Given PTSD's link to memory and stress problems, the affected region is one central to what?
hints.rationale: Memory and stress functions point to the affected region.

hints.prompt: Strategy: which clue best points to the correct region?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Its role in MEMORY and STRESS regulation, shown reduced in PTSD.

choices.label: C
choices.text: That PTSD is anxiety-related in general.

choices.label: D
choices.text: That the question is about the brain.

hints.rationale: The memory-and-stress role is the discriminating clue.

questions.stem: Which of the following is not one of the seven universal emotions described in this chapter?
questions.explanation: Melancholy is not among the universal emotions; the universal emotions include anger, contempt, disgust, fear, happiness, sadness, and surprise.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 10 Q15 = C.

hints.prompt: This item asks which option is NOT among the seven universal emotions. What must the answer be?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An emotion that is NOT on the universal-emotions list.

hints.rationale: The answer must be the option absent from the universal set.

hints.prompt: For a 'which is NOT one of them' question, what is the best strategy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recall the members of the set and pick the option that is not a member.

hints.rationale: Comparing options against the known set isolates the exception.

hints.prompt: Strategy: which clue tells you how to find the correct choice?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The word 'not' — pick the option that is NOT on the universal-emotions list.

hints.rationale: The word 'not' signals you must choose the non-member.
